info.structure: Part 4: Chapter 1, Section 5. Focusing on the cosmic identity between the Udgītha (OM) and the Sun, and internal identity with Prāṇa.
info.format: Devanagari, IAST, English translation, and philosophical commentaries.

sections.title: The Identity of Prāṇa and Āditya through the Udgītha

verses.devanagari: अथ खलु य उद्गीथः स प्रणवो यः प्रणवः स उद्गीथ इति असौ वा आदित्य उद्गीथ एष प्रणव ओमिति ह्येष स्वरन्नेति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha khalu ya udgīthaḥ sa praṇavo yaḥ praṇavaḥ sa udgītha iti asau vā āditya udgītha eṣa praṇava omiti hyeṣa svaranneti || 1 ||
verses.english_translation: Now, verily, that which is Udgītha is Pranava (OM), and that which is Pranava is Udgītha. Yonder Sun is indeed the Udgītha, and He is also the Pranava, for He moves along chanting 'OM'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this verse unifies two distinct Vedic traditions. The Rigveda uses the term *Pranava* to denote the sacred syllable OM, while the Sāmaveda calls it the *Udgītha*. By declaring them absolutely identical, the Upanishad removes any sense of division between the sacrificial paths. Furthermore, the text identifies this phonetic sound with the celestial Sun (*Āditya*). The Sun's daily journey across the sky is allegorized as a continuous acoustic projection of OM, proving that the universe is structurally vibrating with pure conscious energy.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that the complete oneness (*ekatva*) of Udgītha and Pranava shows that different scriptural names point to the same ultimate destination. The Sun moving along and 'chanting OM' signifies that the cosmic bodies are completely subservient to the dynamic will of the Supreme Lord, performing their daily duties as an act of natural worship to Nārāyaṇa.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that *Udgītha* and *Pranava* are two distinct, functional names of the Lord, highlighting His attributes of being 'praised aloud' and 'eternally fresh'. The Sun is called the Udgītha because the indwelling form of Viṣṇu within the solar orb manifests His glory openly to all living beings, serving as the cosmic guide of creation.

verses.devanagari: एतमु एवाहमभ्यगासिषं तस्मान्मम त्वमेकोऽसीति ह कौषीतकिः पुत्रमुवाच रश्मींस्त्वं पर्यावर्तयाद्बहवो वै ते पुत्रा भविष्यन्तीति साधिदैवतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: etamu evāhamabhyagāsiṣaṃ tasmānmama tvameko'sīti ha kauṣītakiḥ putramuvāca raśmīṃstvaṃ paryāvartayādbahavo vai te putrā bhaviṣyantīti sādhidevatam || 2 ||
verses.english_translation: Sage Kauṣītaki once said to his son: 'I meditated on the Sun only as a single object; therefore you are my only son. Do you meditate on each of the multi-colored rays of the Sun individually, so that you may be blessed with many children.' This is the meditation with reference to the cosmic deities (Adhidevata).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara treats this verse as a pragmatic lesson in the physics of meditation. Sage Kauṣītaki realized that his own limited, singular meditation on the collective solar disc resulted in a limited, singular outcome (an only son). He advises his child to diversify his meditation by focusing on the individual solar rays (*raśmīs*), which represent different expressions of the cosmic life-force. In Advaita, this shows how relative meditations yield relative, specific karmic fruits within the realm of nature, contrasting with the absolute meditation on the attributeless Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that this verse confirms the relationship between specialized worship and its rewards. The individual rays are unique energetic limbs (*Vibhūtīs*) of the Lord. Meditating on the richness of God's manifestations expands the soul's capacity to receive blessings, ensuring prosperity and continuity in the material world as an act of divine favor.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that various minor deities reside within the different rays of the Sun, all working under the ultimate command of the Supreme Lord. By meditating on the specific functions of these individual rays, the seeker honors the complex divine order, which naturally results in the multiplication of ancestral blessings and children.

verses.devanagari: अथाध्यात्मं य एवायं मुख्यः प्राणस्तमुद्गीथमुपासीतोमिति ह्युद्गाति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: athādhyātmaṃ ya evāyaṃ mukhyaḥ prāṇastamudgīthamupāsītomiti hyudgāti || 3 ||
verses.english_translation: Now, next, with reference to the individual body (Adhyātma). One should meditate on the chief vital life-force (Mukhya Prāṇa) inside the body as the Udgītha, for it is this breath that silently chants 'OM' within.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara shifts the student's gaze from the sky (*Adhidevata*) directly back into their own physical body (*Adhyātma*). The cosmic Sun is outside, but the internal Sun is the *Mukhya Prāṇa*—the silent, unattached life-force animating our organs. Just as the Sun moves across the cosmos radiating energy, the internal breath flows through the nervous system, sustaining life. Meditating on this internal breath as OM allows the seeker to discover the immutable presence of Consciousness within the biological matrix.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that the internal life-force is the direct, uncorrupted representative of the Lord within the human temple. By transitioning the meditation from the external Sun to the internal breath, the seeker recognizes that God is not a distant sky deity, but the innermost companion who continuously breathes life into their soul.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights this verse as key confirmation of the status of Mukhya Prāṇa. He is the cosmic vehicle of the Lord both outside (as the solar energy) and inside (as the vital life-force). Meditating on the internal breath as the Udgītha means recognizing that our very capacity to think, live, and breathe is a direct gift administered by the highest servant of Viṣṇu.

verses.devanagari: एतमु एवाहमभ्यगासिषं तस्मान्मम त्वमेकोऽसीति ह कौषीतकिः पुत्रमुवाच प्राणांस्त्वं पर्यावर्तयाद्बहवो वै मे पुत्रा भविष्यन्तीति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: etamu evāhamabhyagāsiṣaṃ tasmānmama tvameko'sīti ha kauṣītakiḥ putramuvāca prāṇāṃstvaṃ paryāvartayādbahavo vai me putrā bhaviṣyantīti || 4 ||
verses.english_translation: Sage Kauṣītaki then told his son: 'I meditated on the breath only as a single unified force; therefore you are my only son. Do you meditate on the many specialized modifications of the breath individually, so that you may be blessed with many children.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara correlates this with the previous solar verse. The single vital force manifests as various specialized physiological streams (Prāṇa, Apāna, Vyāna, Udāna, Samāna). Meditating on these individual internal currents reveals the complex tapestry of life. Shankara emphasizes that while these analytical divisions are useful for obtaining specific material rewards, the highest goal remains the ultimate reduction of all distinct modifications back into the single, absolute substrate of Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The modifications of the internal breath represent the beautiful, coordinated craftsmanship of the Lord. Raṅgarāmānuja teaches that by appreciating how each specialized stream of breath maintains health and vitality, the seeker develops a deep sense of gratitude and continuous devotion toward the Supreme Creator.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva points out that the different sub-breaths are governed by distinct companion deities working in perfect union under the central authority of Mukhya Prāṇa. Meditating on them individually honors the complex, flawless hierarchy of the inner universe, leading to harmonious physical existence and abundance.

verses.devanagari: अथ खलु य उद्गीथः स प्रणवो यः प्रणवः स उद्गीथ इति होताषद्नात् हाप्युरुद्गीथं संहरतीत्युद्गीथं संहरतीति ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha khalu ya udgīthaḥ sa praṇavo yaḥ praṇavaḥ sa udgītha iti hotāṣadnāt hāpyurudgīthaṃ saṃharatītyudgīthaṃ saṃharatīti || 5 ||
verses.english_translation: Now, verily, that which is Udgītha is Pranava, and that which is Pranava is Udgītha. Armed with this realization, even if the Hotṛ priest makes an error in his recitation, the Udgātṛ priest corrects and heals it through the power of the Udgītha. Yes, he completely heals it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara highlights the psychological healing power of knowledge. In a complex Vedic sacrifice, if a priest makes an error in the physical chanting, the entire ritual can collapse. However, if the priest possesses this deep internal realization of the identity of OM, Prāṇa, and the Sun, the sheer power of their meditative awareness acts as a spiritual solvent. The inner knowledge corrects and heals the external flaws. In Advaita, this proves that flawless inner wisdom completely neutralizes and transcends any imperfections in the outer, transactional world.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school interprets this healing power as the redemptive grace of the Lord's name. Human actions are naturally prone to errors and imperfections. However, when an action is thoroughly infused with the remembrance of OM—the primary name of God—the Lord forgives the mechanical shortcomings and accepts the offering as perfect, proving that love and devotion transcend ritual technicalities.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the supreme deities guiding the Udgītha and Pranava possess the unique cosmic capacity to overwrite and correct any operational errors made by subordinate human priests. This absolute spiritual security is a direct benefit of understanding the precise cosmic hierarchy, ensuring that the devotee's spiritual progress is never permanently blocked by accidental human mistakes.
